summary: Graham argues that spam filters should go on the offensive by automatically fetching links in suspected spam, turning the spammer's scale against them. This targeted, user-controlled “punish mode” would raise spammers’ costs, reduce sales, and push them toward legitimate unsubscribe practices without requiring new protocols.
key_points: Auto-retrieving links in suspected spam would overload spammer servers and undercut their business model.
The approach reverses spam’s advantage: mass sending would trigger mass hits on spam infrastructure.
To avoid collateral damage, retrieval should target only blacklisted, human-verified spam sites.
Widespread use could force spammers to include working unsubscribe links, effectively becoming auto-unsubscribing filters.
The scheme can be tested incrementally without changing email protocols.
money_quotes: If popular email clients did this in order to filter spam, the spammer's servers would take a serious pounding.
This isn't just amusing; it would be hard to imagine a more perfectly targeted counterattack on spammers.
The huge volume of the spam, which has so far worked in the spammer's favor, would now work against him, like a branch snapping back in his face.
Pump out a million emails an hour, get a million hits an hour on your servers.
In this scenario, spam would, like OS crashes, viruses, and popups, become one of those plagues that only afflict people who don't bother to use the right software.
key_insight: Turning spam filters into active link-fetchers could make spamming economically self-defeating by converting mass email volume into crippling load on spammer servers.
